data.title: Sandisk: I See A Path To $1000 Based On 2027 EPS Forecasts (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Sandisk Corporation (SNDK) has taken the market by storm, staging a face melting 1000% rally.

But the way I see it, this stock could quite easily double in the next 18 months, with $1000 a likely price target.

I reached this conclusion by running some forecasts based on earnings estimates and potential P/E ratios.

With the recent selloff, technicals are resetting, and I see a pretty clear accumulation structure.

Support at $480 is strong, and this would offer a perfect entry point

Sandisk looks Cheap Here

Yes, Sandisk has rallied over 1000% since its spinoff. I covered exactly why in my last article.

Not only is Sandisk a huge beneficiary of the memory shortage, but it is also potentially an even better play than Micron (MU)

But to understand this crazy rally, one only needs to look at the earnings estimates.

Sandisk is expected to grow earnings by 1000% in 2026, and then double EPS in 2027. This is crazy, and it makes valuing the company based on traditional fundamentals quite difficult, but we can certainly try.

In order to do this, let’s fast forward to June 2027, where EPS should be close to $80. At this point, growth should have normalized.

In fact, a consensus of 8 analysts has EPS declining by nearly 10% in 2028. But what would be a fair price at that point?

Scenario: $81 EPS 2027

Growth Rate Assumption

Implied P/E to have PEG=1

"Fair" Price

Current Market

103% (2027 Forecast)

~7x

$565

Conservative Case

20% (Long-term)

20x

$1,620

Analyst "High"

~12.5% (Relative)

12.5x

$1,012

This really depends on how we view growth longer term.

As I mentioned in my recent Micron article, PEG is perhaps one of the best metrics to see if a tech company is undervalued.

A fairly valued company should traditionally have a P/E ratio that matches the forecasted or recent growth rate, in order to balance out the PEG at 1.

At today’s price and forecast, as we can see above, this implies SNDK would trade at a 7 PE in 2027. But this doesn’t seem realistic at all.

Even if growth does slow down for a year or two, I believe Sandisk, like Micron, is benefiting from a secular growth trend.

Assuming a longer-term growth rate of 20% and P/E of 20, gives us a target price of $1,620. This is likely on the higher side.

Ultimately, if we apply a P/E of around 12, which is slightly below today’s forward PE of 1/5, we get a target price of just over $1000.

This is in line with the price target from UBS, and very reasonable given comparable industry multiples and the current valuation.

Again, this could vary a lot depending on how growth looks after two years, but this is fairly conservative P/E in my opinion.

Technicals Are Re-Setting

The other key development since my last update is that we’re seeing a bullish consolidation and resetting of the technicals.

While the broader market sells off, Sandisk is holding in a tight range, consolidating as it did back in Q4 of 2025. Meanwhile, the daily RSI is back near neutral levels.

Sandisk has broken below the 20 EMA, and it looks ready to test support just above $500, which is the 50 EMA and an area of volume support.

This will likely be an ideal entry point, just like it was back in November.

Risks

But even though Sandisk looks like a slam dunk, there are still considerable investment risks.

Sandisk is a newly spun-off corporation, and there’s still a lot we don't know about the business and management.

We also have significant execution risk, and with expectations so high, any miss could really lead to a re-rating of the company.

To add to this, market sentiment around AI has been pretty bearish in the last few months, and we may need to see a wider correction before we can see a reversal and new highs.

Focusing on the technicals, it's worth noting that if we do break below the 50 EMA, we don’t have much support until the 200 EMA at $250, so there’s some potential downside and volatility ahead.

Final Thoughts

I really like Sandisk at this price, and I believe this stock could easily double in the next 18 months.

This makes sense when we look at valuation, and there’s room for this to go even beyond $1000.

The technicals also look bullish to me, for the time being, but this could quickly change.

data.title: Surviving 'Epic Fury' And The Asian Stock Market Crash
data.description:
Colonel Kilgore enjoyed the smell of morning napalm in "Apocalypse Now" since it reflected triumph. Today on institutional trading floors on Wall Street, the air reeks of volatility, but only a faint scent of margin calls seems to be burning off traders’ terminal screens. Not only did “Operation Epic Fury” in the Middle East destroy Iran's radar systems, it also destroyed tens of billions of dollars in leveraged bets, eliminating the illusion of comfort that had permeated the markets. Spring Cleaning (Also applicable since we are entering the warmest season). There are two different stories told by the collapsing of Asian markets and the perceived/illusory strength of U.S. stocks.

Here I will attempt to connect the dots. I may go against the mainstream media, but this is what I believe. I am employed by an asset management company and have access to high-level market data through Bloomberg Terminals and reporting from sell-side analysts from all around the world. However, I always form my own opinion, using capital flow to differentiate between fundamentals and algorithmic hysteria. I strive to remain objective and do not want to sell you one story versus the other here, just the facts.

The Bureaucratic Blockade and the “Crude Tax"

Stock exchanges are typically impervious to the threat of geopolitical crises. We observed this between 2023 and 2025 and again at the beginning of 2026 with the Venezuelan and Greenlandian crises. Stock exchanges are generally apathetic towards conflict or wars until it affects an economy’s ability to generate cash flow. Pure geopolitics will never result in a bear market. The "preventive strike" on Iran's nuclear and command structure immediately resulted in retaliatory strikes by Iran on Qatar, Bahrain, and Oman. These retaliations resulted in a disruption of the worldwide energy balance that was already delicately balanced.

The most destructive weapon of economic warfare in this crisis is bureaucracy. I covered this in my last two analyses (Part I and Part II). The Strait of Hormuz, a 39-kilometer channel through which 21% of the world’s total petroleum consumption (21 million barrels per day) is delivered, is now blocked. 7 Major Marine Insurance Associations have withdrawn coverage for War Risk in the Persian Gulf. Not a single shipping company is willing to take their tankers across the Persian Gulf as long as they do not have insurance. This "Administrative Blockade" has created a floating parking lot for over 700 ships.

Late yesterday afternoon, the president promised a U.S. Navy escort for tankers. That caused a small drop in oil price when the news was released but did little to assuage institutional investors. While an armed escort protects against a physical attack, it does nothing to stop an attacking drone from starting a fire at an operating refinery.

In just 48 hours, Brent crude has increased 12%, to $82/barrel. Goldman Sachs estimates that a prolonged closure would add a $15 premium to the structural fair value of a barrel of oil. If oil stays above $100/bbl, then trucking logistics costs will increase by anywhere from 10% to 18%. The margin compression of corporations will be taxed heavily either through destruction of demand or the burn rate of revenue through absorbing those costs.

Bloomberg Economics produced a chart showing how severe this would be. For example, if we were to have a prolonged, high-intensity conflict, the price of crude oil could go to $108/bbl. At that price, any reasonable model of multiple expansion that is currently supporting the current bull cycle would cease to exist.

Asian Massacre and the AI Bubble

When Western investors were focused on the Middle East, the biggest financial crash was taking place in Seoul, Tokyo, and Hong Kong. The iShares MSCI Pacific ex-Japan ETF (EPP) dropped up to 4.5 percent during one day, while the Korean Stock Exchange (Kospi Index) plummeted an incredible 7.2 percent in a single trading session. A week later, the Kospi index would fall another 6.2 percent, eliminating $430 billion in market value in less than two days.

In retail investor circles, it is commonly stated that the reason for the collapse was Asia's dependency on imported petroleum products. Data on institutional flow activity reveals the opposite. Both Japan and China have sufficient hydrocarbon reserves to maintain domestic economic stability for at least several months, and in the case of China, possibly even one full year. It is not a problem with petroleum products that caused the collapse, but the most severe speculative bubble since the last major speculative bubble occurred at the end of the dot-com era.

Country Day
Japan 254
South Korea 208
China 200
India 74
Thailand 61
Philippines 60
Indonesia 20
Vietnam 15
Cambogia No Data

Before the war, investors worldwide flocked into North Asian stocks, particularly those of companies in the AI hardware space, specifically Samsung Electronics Co. (SSNLF), SK Hynix Inc. (HXSCF), etc., with price expectations that these would continue to grow indefinitely.

These funds continued to be poured into the space. The iShares MSCI South Korea ETF (EWY) received an unprecedented amount of money as it recorded the largest outflows in a 5-day period in its 25-year history of $1.77 billion before the war. Retail investors were highly risky and also pushed margin borrowing to record levels because of a very loose lending environment.

Hostilities broke out and were the trigger that set off the energy price rise, which supported the U.S. dollar (DXY), pushed up interest rates, and thus put the leveraged positions of Asian speculative investors at risk. Once banks started issuing merciless margin calls, they cut off the liquidity that allowed their trading accounts to be able to recover from the losses caused by these margin calls. So the winners in the semiconductor trade dumped their holdings for a profit.

These trades, as shown in the Kospi chart above, represent an example of what is known as "algorithmic dysfunction." These are not simply a result of a "technical retracement," but rather the forced liquidation of all the crowded positions taken on by investors who were long in semiconductors and short in software and were unable to meet their margin calls. And because the long and short trades were done simultaneously, there was "double damage" done to their portfolios.

The Death of Safe Havens and the Dominance of the Dollar

With global financial markets becoming so complex that there are fewer places to hide than ever before, finding a safe haven has become an act of faith rather than an eventuality. The financial dynamics caused by the current Middle East crisis have turned what were once considered safe havens into liquidity traps, except for one.

U.S. Treasury Prices Plummet While Yields Soar

iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) prices plummeted as the yields on U.S. Treasuries rose sharply. The 10-Year Treasury rate broke the 4.06% barrier and closed at 4.11%. The two-year treasury suffered a sharp 12 basis point rise to 3.59%.

The yields on these U.S. Treasury securities increased during the crisis because of two factors:

  • The fear of inflation: When there is a large rise in energy costs, the actual or real value of a fixed-rate bond decreases. For example, if the price of crude oil increases by 50%, then it is estimated that the rate of inflation in the United States will increase by approximately 1.4 percent (the increase in the European Union will be slightly less at 1.1 percent, and in Japan at 1.2 percent). Therefore, when there is an expectation of increasing inflation, the Federal Reserve Board does not have the option of cutting interest rates to help purchase U.S. government securities.
  • Increased fiscal risk and deficit: The energy crisis has also been exacerbated by a domestic political concern for the U.S. administration. The U.S. Supreme Court decided against President Donald Trump's suggested tariffs, therefore lowering the predicted tax income suggested by the White House. Additionally, with the prospect of additional military spending due to the growing Asian and Middle Eastern conflicts, there is a greater fear among investors of an uncontrollable national debt and deficit, and therefore, a significant decrease in the attractiveness of U.S. government securities.

Gold and Silver

Gold has always represented a perfect example of a safe haven, but its behavior toward this economic shock has been volatile, unpredictable, and erratic – three traits no investor would ever want to see in a hedge product.

  • The price of gold initially surged upward from the time of the announcement to a high of $5,419 an ounce before collapsing back down to $5,261 and recovering slightly to close at $5,337 per ounce; however, the volatility experienced by silver was far worse than that of gold as it dropped approximately 7 percent.
  • Gold falls during war because the price of gold has grown so financially sensitive that the commodity has become an ideal target for both tactical speculation and opportunistic investment strategies. During times of major equity market risk-off, as we have witnessed with the collapse of Asian markets, speculative positions are being sold out due to margin calls resulting from a lack of liquidity. The funds selling their positions are doing so based on need rather than choice. Since gold and silver had reached all-time highs and provided massive returns, these commodities became prime targets for selling off to raise cash to meet margin calls resulting from the collapse of technology equities. Historically, gold and silver were considered non-correlated assets; however, due to the extreme amount of money being raised through the sale of gold and silver in order to raise cash to meet margin calls, the two assets have exhibited a lethal positive correlation. When liquidity becomes extremely tight, all assets will experience significant declines.

The Hegemony of the Dollar King (The Last Bastion)

The U.S. Dollar has become the only remaining unchallenged source of global life support.

Wall Street's False Security

While the Eastern front was undergoing bloodshed, the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), and the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) held steady and demonstrated unrelenting strength. Not only did the U.S. equity market survive the assault, it also triggered a ferocious rebound. The S&P 500 rallied 0.7%, while the Nasdaq 100 surged 1.3%, and was primarily led by tech stocks.

There were two catalysts for the buyers.

  1. Rumors that Iran may have initiated diplomatic overtures with the intent to broker a cease-fire to the conflict (subsequently denied).
  2. the "Goldilocks" macroeconomic numbers. The U.S. ISM Services Index, which had been expanding at a faster-than-expected rate since mid-2022, jumped to 56.1 for the highest growth rate in years.

Historically, the other major risk factor to the financial markets is monetary. Every $10 per barrel increase in crude oil has historically pushed the CPI upward approximately 20-30 basis points. Since the CPI remains stubbornly high at 2.7% - 2.9%, a new shock to energy prices effectively limits the ability of the Fed to take action.

Bottom Line

If the oil price actually approaches $100, we have a very serious problem.

A severe lesson is provided by the forced sector rotation in Asia: In extremely polarized markets, the greatest risk is associated with extreme positioning. The second round effects from inflation of energy prices have still not been accounted for by Wall Street. Betting on an intraday rebound of the S&P 500 by betting on a Fed rescue is a mathematical loser because inflation has eliminated the possibility of a Fed rate cut.

I would recommend holding off from investing in the S&P 500 at this time. As I have said in many analyses, the most beneficial thing you can do right now is "do nothing" and wait. There is simply no justification for committing additional capital at current multiples with both a compressed equity risk premium and increasing real interest rates.

Margin calls have briefly destroyed Asian Markets, but after that, we can identify long-term strategic entry points from the price dislocations that will arise once selling volumes normalize. Waiting is my personal choice.

Liquidity isn't a cost; rather, I think you should now view it as the only tactical (defensive) tool to take advantage of the price anomalies caused by the forced liquidation of assets in the coming quarters. Many investors think central banks will come to their rescue. It's possible, and I don't think the Fed will ever make another 2008-style mistake. Regardless, I don't want to suffer this volatility; rather, I want to manage it.

What about you?

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Nvidia: Abnormal Returns Are A Thing From The Past
data.description:
Last week, Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) released its Q4 2026 earnings, and most market participants were up in arms with the negative share price response. To many the combination of a strong quarterly report and a share price drop was interpreted as a strong buying opportunity and hopes that the market is somehow being irrational about one of the most followed companies of our day.

After all, NVDA had yet another record-breaking quarter and the stock is still down by around 6% over the past 5-day period.

It might be disappointing seeing the underlying business prosper while the stock price is being essentially flat over the past 6-month period, but that's the reality when investing in stocks that are trading at prohibitively high multiples.

The Q4 report and the reaction that followed were an exact repetition from Nvidia's previous report, which did not translate into a meaningful boost for the share price in spite of the strong revenue growth and margin improvements. That is why, back in November, I warned that investors should distinguish between Nvidia's as a business and NVDA share price.

The Telling Signs

If we zoom-out by just one year, then the recent drop of NVDA looks less as an irrational movement and more as a normal course of events after results got priced-in about a year earlier.

As a matter of fact, earnings growth in FY 2026 is roughly in-line with NVDA share price performance over the same period.

Earnings surprises above the analysts' estimates have also come down gradually in recent years and over the past few quarters have been at mid-single digits which suggests that the market forecasts are now aligned.

Another telling sign that investors should not take NVDA share price rally in the first half of calendar 2025 as a given was the major drop in trading volumes. This is usually associated with lack of conviction and a potential pullback later on as investors adjust their views.

As a result, it is not surprising that we now see NVDA trading at exactly the same multiples from a year ago. This does not indicate that the business performance is worsening, but rather that the market is now in a much better position to accurately forecast earnings growth in the coming year.

Strong Narratives, Predictable Performance

What instantly became clear during the earnings release was that there's no slowdown of Nvidia's business momentum. The company remains as the "Inference King" with no immediate threat of competition and sustained demand from data centers.

Demand for Blackwell architecture remains strong with even older architectures showing encouraging results which is a clear sign that the sector is still faced with temporary capacity constrains which could often result in short-term abnormalities in terms of pricing.

Q4 data center revenue of $62 billion increased 75% year-over-year and 22% sequentially, driven primarily by sustained strength in Blackwell and the Blackwell Ultra ramp. With NVIDIA infrastructure in high demand, even Hopper and much of the 6-year-old Ampere-based products are sold out in the cloud.

Source: NVDA Q4 2026 Earnings Transcript.

Nvidia's management also highlighted the growing opportunity in networking, which is an essential part of the data center business. Revenue in networking grew by a staggering 350% during the last quarter and that's impressive, albeit once again - mostly anticipated at this point. CEO Jen-Hsun Huang hinted that NVDA will be leveraging its competitive positioning in the space and that's definitely good news for investors.

And I think that we're probably the largest Ethernet networking company in the world today and surely will be soon.

Source: NVDA Q4 2026 Earnings Transcript.

Recent quarterly results from Cisco (CSCO) have also shown a notable pickup in demand from hyperscalers for networking solutions.

AI infrastructure orders taken from hyperscalers totalled $2.1 billion in Q2 compared to $1.3 billion just last quarter and equal to the total orders taken in all of fiscal year '25, marking another significant acceleration in growth across our silicon, systems and optics.

Source: CSCO Q2 2026 Earnings Transcript.

As a result, NVDA's guidance for the current quarter involves a total revenue of $78bn, up from $44bn in the prior period.

This assumes around 75% year-on-year growth in the first quarter of fiscal year 2027 and right now that's above the consensus forward revenue growth estimate of 52%.

This divergence might seem counterintuitive, especially when we consider that so far hyperscalers' capital expenditures show no signs of cooling off in the first months of 2026.

Analyst expectations for 2026 CapEx across the top 5 cloud providers and hyperscalers who collectively account for a little over 50% of our data center revenue are up nearly $120 billion since the start of the year and approaching $700 billion. We continue to expect the transition of classic data center workloads to GPU accelerated computing and the use of AI to enhance today's hyperscale workloads and contribute toward roughly half of our long-term opportunity.

Source: NVDA Q4 2026 Earnings Transcript.

The broader expectations are for hyperscalers capex to increase considerably in 2026, but many investors are already looking ahead for what's to come after that peak in spending.

So far, on an aggregated quarterly basis, we do not see any major red flags and Q1 2026 numbers will be much anticipated after overall spend in the last quarter of 2025 noted a slight cool-off to 71% year-on-year growth.

Some analysts are pointing to the hyperscalers' growing needs to finance their growth through debt as internally generated cash flow is no longer enough fuel the rapid development of computing capacity.

That's hardly a reason to be concerned given the strong balance sheet of these companies, but it is a sign that the current spending spree is unlikely to grow further and capex growth would have to come down in the coming years.

The main issue for NVDA here is that even a slight ease in capex growth by the hyperscalers could have major consequences. The now lower price/earnings multiple does take into account the increased risk of slower revenue growth. A P/E multiple of 22 on a forward basis might appear quite low for a company like NVDA, but that low multiple could be misleading.

The extraordinary increase in capex by the hyperscalers last year and the lack of spare capacity by the semiconductor foundries has resulted in significant price increases for Nvidia chips. That is why, the commentary extract above that even Hopper and Ampere-based products are selling quite well is not surprising.

Thus, it is reasonable to assume that NVDA gross profitability is temporary elevated until the shortages are resolved. Right now gross margins have settled at around 75% (see the graph below) and most recent guidance for Q1 2027 confirmed that the prior highs of 78% and above are unlikely to be achieved again. Fixed cost efficiencies have allowed NVDA to retain its operating margins close to record-highs, but this might not last if gross margins continue to normalize in the coming years.

The market is now preemptively taking measures against the very high probability scenario of NVDA gross margins normalizing in the coming years. While operating margins are unlikely to fall very steeply on the back of fixed cost efficiencies, they will be subject to pressure too. In such an event, NVDA's EPS growth would suffer tremendously, even if revenue growth remains strong.

Whether or not the current and forward P/E multiples accurately reflect what's to come is debatable, but given the risks outlined above a forward multiple of 22 does not necessarily mean that NVDA is undervalued. More likely than not, we will witness more volatility in calendar 2026, but by taking pre-emptive measures against margin pressures and expected cool-off of hyperscalers' capex growth is now the most sensible course action by the market.

Conclusion

Nvidia's share price drop following the latest quarterly release was both anticipated and sensible. The stock's strong rally in the first half of 2025 has already accounted for years of growth, without properly addressing some risks. Market participants are already looking ahead and anticipating a degree of normalization when it comes to cloud infrastructure spending and this is now being reflected within NVDA's performance. While volatility is to be expected in 2026, investors should get used to the stock's lower valuation multiples.

data.title: Vistra: Time To Wait, Bright Future
data.description:
The integrated retail electricity and power generation company Vistra Corp. (VST) just released a mixed Q4 earnings report that has led to a 13% drop in its stock price over the last week. I gave VST a buy rating after its Q3 earnings, in which the stock remained relatively flat for months until its recent correction here in early March.

My prior buy rating was based on earnings and operational growth, cash generation, and acquisitional expansion. This perspective does not change much for me and remains mostly positive; however, valuation continues to rise, and operating costs are scaling nearly as fast as adjusted EBITDA.

I am changing my rating for VST to a hold.

Q4 Snapshot

VST is a large U.S. power company that has power plants with natural gas, coal, nuclear, and solar. They provide to residential, commercial, and industrial customers through dozens of states. Natural gas and coal make up the majority of their production, but they are seeing advancements in “cleaner” energy spaces like nuclear.

Adjusted EPS for Q4 missed marginally, arriving at $2.34, also slightly down YoY. Sales were opposite, down from Q3 but up by about $500 million YoY, reaching $4.58 billion for the quarter. VST had $785 million in cash or cash equivalents at the end of the quarter, up largely from last quarter.

Net income is down big from 2024, which I also noted after last quarter's report. This is largely due to unrealized derivative positions and losses from hedging transactions. Management again notes that once commodity prices increase, this significantly helps their portfolio in the future.

Adjusted EBITDA was $5.91 billion for 2025, up from $5.64 billion the year prior. For utility companies like VST, adjusted EBITDA is often the “go-to” metric for evaluating the business because it captures operational performance and cash flow. It’s a good sign that operations continue to generate more profits each year for VST, as energy prices do fluctuate.

Growth Drivers

Improving total generation capacity and output is going to be a key driver to growth moving forward, as demand for electric consumption is anticipated to remain strong. Producing more megawatt-hours (MWH) to sell typically helps raise operational margins, as many fixed costs remain steady.

VST is working on generation gains through several acquisitions. First Lotus Infrastructure Partners in Q4 2025 and now Cogentrix in Q1 2026. Lotus has been finalized, and VST is beginning to see the additions in sales and generation output. The Cogentrix deal is subject to close later in 2026 but is hoped to provide an additional 5,500 MW of modern natural gas generation assets. Generation output grew by about 7% this past year, as seen above, which should continue to expand through acquisition and organically.

Another growth driver that just remains a little further out is the nuclear power purchase agreements with AWS and Meta. VST has recently announced a 20-year power purchase agreement with Meta for more than 2,600 MW of zero-carbon nuclear energy. They had already agreed with AWS to supply 1,200 MW of carbon-free electricity to its Comanche Peak Nuclear Power Plant for another 20-year deal. These deals won’t be fully ramped up for at least several years but will eventually provide great long-term and stable revenue.

Management has given a 2026 adjusted EBITDA forecast, starting with a projection of $6.8-$7.6 billion as the range. Even the very low end of this forecast would be a 17% increase from 2025, with hopes of much more. Management's confidence in EBITDA growth is enlightening, especially considering the growth that is coming from the power purchase agreements in the coming years. I think this stock has real potential to rise eventually, but I don’t know if the time is now.

Risks For VST

Valuation is the No. 1 red flag for VST. PE has kept rising over the last year and now sits at 76x for the TTM, which is almost 4x the sector median and way above other industry-specific peers. The current price-to-book of 21x is incredibly elevated. The premium is the key element why I can’t give the stock a buy again right now. We need some substantial earnings growth first. I do expect this, but I think it could still take another year or two for the acquisitions to synergize and then even longer for the nuclear agreements to finalize and help toward VST's bottom line.

Capital expenditures are rising, which is OK with me, as they have earned the right to spend their nice cash generation. It’s the rise in operating costs that worries me at the moment. Between regular operation, maintenance, fuel, SG&A, and impairment charges, total costs working against growing sales are adding up almost as fast. I do think as generation capacity gains, through fixed costs, this should be able to slow down, but I would like to see this for a few quarters first.

Conclusion

Operating margins have been flat since 2023 for VST as costs have kept pace with generation and revenue gains. There has been earnings growth, but it has been a little sporadic, which is expected for a utility power producer in their space. The growth in their retail segment is more predictable, and the future income from the long-term nuclear contracts will really help subside some commodity price volatility. I think that the inflated valuation and operating costs are too much at the moment. The future outlook is very healthy in an industry with growing demand. Waiting is best for now with VST.

data.title: Axia: How To Value Brazilian Energy/Utilities
data.description:
The rise of volatility has caused me, unlike other investors, to find safety and security in other geographies and investments that are not as "crowded" as the investments you're likely to generally find on the market today. The risk of going into such "crowded" investments is quite obvious and can be highlighted by looking at what is currently going on in nations like South Korea. A crowded investment can be likened to a boat with everyone on "one side" of that boat. When a market shock is triggered, everyone is quite literally trying to exit at once. Another apt metaphor is that of musical chairs – there simply won't be enough chairs. Such events easily create crashes of double digits easily, where investors and markets are trying to find buyers for the equity, which often overnight is suddenly worth a lot less on the open market than they thought it was.

Secondly, investment crisis correlation. In a crowded investment, you can be invested in something like utilities, and then something like AI crashes, and even your utilities (a supposedly safe investment) start to crash as well. This is the difficulty with crowded trading, requiring investors to do things like cover substantial margin calls. This is best compared to a pestilence or contagion where bad things in one investment start "infecting" other investments, even though the actual correlation might be very low. Again, South Korea is a good investment right now. There are investments being liquidated where the underlying sectors are entirely healthy. But it doesn't matter – when the market crash starts, logic is out the door.

This is why my strategy is to never be in that position in the first place. In my "search for value" and undervalued assets this day, I am taking you to Brazil. I will be looking at one of the largest utilities in the entire world, AXIA Energia SA (AXIA).

What is Axia SA, and what does it do?

AXIA, known to some of you probably as Electrobras, is the largest utility in Brazil and one of the largest in the entire world overall. It's been known as AXIA for a short time—about four to six months. The reason for this rebranding is that it was privatized about four years ago. I've been keeping an eye on it since to find out at what point I believe we can go in and get some "bang for our buck."

Generally speaking, I'm positive about investing in previously state-owned enterprises. The best examples would be Norway, but Sweden also has many of them, from telecommunications to others where there is potential for profit. The trick is not to overestimate their upward potential or surge potential. In my experience, despite being privatized, these companies tend to remain with the state-owned operational mindset for quite some time. It takes a long period, usually more than 1-2 management changes, to change a company culture. My best "cure" for this is to invest cheaply.

So that's what I'm trying to do here.

AXIA generates about 35% of the total electricity that Brazil needs and has about 45% of its transmission network. Its asset portfolio is very, very impressive. It has over 44 hydro plants, as well as solar plants, wind power, and thermal plants. The company is already very high in nameplate renewable capacity – and this is before even mentioning over 73,000 km of high-voltage transmission cable across the nation.

I don't want to go into how much it would cost, in a theoretical perspective, to replace this infrastructure – but let's just say it could not be done today at any cost approaching the historical cost basis (even accounted for today's monetary value) to any degree. AXIA has, to put it clearly to you, a 100% renewable and clean energy portfolio.

Given the scope and size of its operations, this is an incredibly attractive set of fundamentals.

Furthermore, and this is where we go in the state-owned legacy – it also operates mining assets. Its assets involve gold, copper, graphite, rare earth, and copper. It's not just "one asset" either, no. AXIA owns 22 permits covering over 40,000 hectares. This makes AXIA not only a miner "in spirit," but it also makes the company a miner in actuality.

We therefore have the somewhat "odd" mixture of it being a utility and a miner - two sectors that typically go very poorly together.

The company only has a BB rating and is not IG-rated. But I would not characterize AXIA as being significantly impacted or risky from its debt/leverage.

Why, given its BB?

Because the company has successfully executed the transition from public to private. It has delevered some and is delevering further. Furthermore, as of Q4, the company holds over 28.3B in cash and securities. This means that any near-term funding needs are met with cash. Short-term obligations through 2028 are less than 13B, and until 2028E, long-term debt is about 26.1B. Much of the company's debt is indexed to inflation, giving it interest rate protection. While we cannot yet compare AXIA to a European or US-based utility, the advantage here is clear enough. Net leverage is expected to stabilize at about 3x in 2028E, which would make it even more attractive.

So, we can check off leverage and cash as a bit of a non-concern for the time being. We can state that the company's portfolio is really quite attractive for what it represents. The mining assets were probably the biggest head-scratcher for me in this one. I expected, when I started researching the company, that AXIA would sell them off due to operational efficiency, but no such announcement. Instead, AXIA is selling off nuclear and thermal assets.

Instead of selling mining assets, the company is busily acquiring more of these assets. It's expanding Bom Jardim Copper; it's engaging partnerships for new mineral explorations.

So, the way to view AXIA is as a mixed utility/miner of all things. What does that mean for the way I view the company from a risk-reward perspective?

Going by my expertise in utilities, the utility will act as the "cash printer." Cash will continue to flow regardless of mining levels because revenues are not only regulated but fixed, regardless of the consumption of energy in Brazil. This means that AXIA has a very attractive "floor" for its operations. This is also the key consideration that makes AXIA's existing debt of such little concern to me. The company could literally pay off the debt with the secured earnings from its utility over the next decade and likely become debt-free if it wanted.

That's utility.

Then we have the "black sheep", by which I mean in terms of volatility. The mining. As we all know, mining operations are correlated to very risky exploration trends and commodity pricing trends. The current trends for these levels, given the current macro, are obviously positive. This can change going forward. If nickel or copper crashed, the mining division's income would crash.

The argument that AXIA makes is that by mining its own green minerals/metals and rare earths, it's hedging its own operations. It can literally deliver the copper it needs for its expansion of power cables. This offsets energy material cost increases across the world. This is not a bad argument. The mining operation is also of lower overall cost than its utility side (which has very high CapEx).

The mix of these two sectors should, therefore, theoretically, drive valuations to behave as a mix of a growth stock and a defensive utility. The trick for this, and I will be very clear about this, is to properly evaluate this and to make sure that you either understand how the market is evaluating it and/or avoid it when it's being evaluated in a way that I would consider improper.

Let's look at how such a valuation would look.

AXIA Energias SA - Valuation is very tricky

Valuation has a lot of guesswork. Despite all the positives I've mentioned above, AXIA is volatile. It misses targets more than 50% of the time negatively, beats them often, and estimates are only hit about 9% of the time with a 10% margin of error. This means that in terms of forecastability, AXIA behaves much more like a mining stock than a utility – a utility usually always hits its targets within a 10% margin of error. Only something unforeseen would dislodge this trend. This has been further made complex by a recent massive corporate restructuring.

In terms of P/B, the company is somewhat close to its peers – 1.1-1.2x seems to be the current valuation here, and this is close to other utilities (some argue a discount; I believe this discount to be justified given the relative volatility in the geography). We cannot use EV/EBITDA, and P/E is at about 25-27x on a TTM basis, which would imply an extremely high overall valuation. An SOTP approach, the third I try, is relevant due to the share class migration the company has announced, which might unlock a premium.

The current suggestion for the company is moving all of its pref shares, native tickers PNB1 and PNA1, into one single common share at a 1.1x ratio (for every 1 share). The pref shares obviously had payout/dividend priority with a 10% premium. The current ADR, a native 1x, trades at $11.28/share, and looking at the native share price targets, we have an analyst average from 10 analysts that actually arrives close to the target we have for the company today. 7 of the 10 are still at a "BUY", 3 at a "HOLD".

The way I interpret this is that there's a significant expectation for the mining arm here, due to the current macro, which is driving this supposed undervaluation. I would be more careful than this - AXIA has risen more than 100% in 2025. While a conservative DCF would imply a higher PT, somewhere at $14-$16.

The two main risk factors that I try to include in my approach are as follows.

First, we have political instability. 2026 is an election year in Brazil, and I believe we might get the company cheaper than we're seeing it here, because typically elections in Brazil are sources of some market instability. Second, the current Iran conflict is driving up oil prices and volatility, which is also causing broader weakness in South American and LATAM stock markets. I view this as a broader opportunity, but I do not believe that we've yet reached a point where this company makes compelling sense to buy here.

I will start my coverage of the company by applying a sector-like discount to 14-16x P/E to the company, which aligns it with historical averages and peers. I move back the company's EV/EBITDA towards more normalized levels. But under current estimates, my target of about 50 BRL would come to roughly $10.5/share for the company ADR. This is, in my view, pricing for the worst-case scenario for the Novo Mercado (that's the share class) migration on the 1st of April, and it also includes what I view as energy volatility (which I consider to be very likely here).

For that reason, my introductory PT is $10.4/share, and I put an FV of about $12.5/share. That makes the company a "HOLD" here.

Thesis

I see AXIA Energias as a very fundamentally attractive Brazilian mining/utility company if bought at "cheap" prices. The combination of these two sectors and their respective upsides and fundamentals can indeed create a virtuous cycle, provided that your cost basis is low enough.

For all the current regulatory and company changes that are happening, I do, however, consider the price for the company slightly elevated. When combined with regional risks and current downside potential from the geopolitical situation as well as capital flight from emerging markets, I fail to see the downside of waiting for a better entry here.

I price the company conservatively at 14-16x P/E here, which comes to about $10.5/share for the ADR and 50 BRL for the native, with an FV of about $12.5/share.

That makes the company a "HOLD" here.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this and a valuation target of 50 BRL/share, where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of March 2026.

data.title: The Sun Will Shine On Moderna Again - Initiating With A Buy
data.description:
Moderna, Inc. (MRNA) was one of the biggest names of the COVID era as their SpikeVax vaccine was considered one of the most effective along with Pfizer (PFE) and BioNTech's (BNTX) Comirnaty. However, this hype has died down significantly. As you can see in the below chart, the stock actually has strong 52 week gains but it should also be noted that despite this rally, Moderna is still miles below its 2021 highs.

Today, I'm initiating coverage on this leading mRNA company with a look at their latest earnings, recent developments, and valuation.

Below, it is shown that despite the continuing decline in revenues, the worst seems to be over. The company is controlling costs and expenses quite well, and they are narrowing their losses. Looking forward, strong growth drivers are in place, and guidance for 2026 is robust. The page is finally turning for Moderna, and despite some risks that will be discussed, the discounted P/B ratio provides a good amount of opportunity for investors looking to buy this mRNA leader.

Therefore, I have decided to initiate the stock at a buy rating.

Multiyear Growth Drivers In Place

Before looking at the results for their 2025 Q4, let's take a big-picture look at where things are going for Moderna. While the demand for COVID vaccines is nowhere near as strong as back during the pandemic era, the company does have notable growth drivers that should help to reaccelerate their business momentum over the next few years.

On the vaccine side of things, their mNEXSPIKE is expected to be a driver for 2026 as approval has already been granted in Canada and Australia. This new vaccine that's targeted for high-risk individuals is likely to grab a good part of the market, so it is set to boost growth for Moderna.

Looking further out, the flu vaccine has been accepted for review in various countries, while the filing for the flu + COVID combination vaccine is also under review in some jurisdictions. Just a few days ago, Jonathan Block of Seeking Alpha wrote that "the European Medicines Agency has recommended that Moderna's combination COVID-19 and influenza vaccine receive marketing authorization." This is obviously a welcomed sign and shows that they are on track for launch. Lastly, Norovirus is in Phase 3 testing.

As you can see above, in addition to these all-important infectious disease vaccines, geographic expansion and development of therapeutics are set to provide a further boost to growth, so I would say that the company is well positioned for the next few years. The push to implement mRNA technology into oncology and rare disease therapeutics is an encouraging sign.

Revenue Declines Slow

In terms of the company's financial results, things still don't seem overly pretty, but there may be some positives upon a closer look. For the top line, Moderna generated total revenues of $678 million, down by 30% YoY. While that is still highly undesirable, note that in the past few quarters there have been signs of stabilization in their revenue declines after YoY drops of over 80% were seen in 2023 and 2024. This may suggest that the worst is behind them. The slowing of declines in Q4 from Q3 levels is of course another good sign. Lastly, the company beat analyst expectations by $54.07 million, so top-line results are better than feared.

U.S. Reliance Is Now A Risk

In the fourth quarter, Moderna actually saw more international revenues than U.S. revenues. However, for the full year 2025, they still had significantly more contributions from U.S. revenues. Usually, this isn't a risk, but under the Trump Administration, this has become something that investors should have in mind.

In mid-February, it was reported that RFK Jr.'s vaccine policy changes are being challenged in court. At this point, it is obviously unknown which side the court will rule in favor of, so there is a great deal of uncertainty for vaccine makers in the American market. For Moderna, this means that the environment in their most important market is not the most desirable.

That said, just days later on Feb. 18, it was reported that the U.S. FDA had accepted Moderna's mRNA-1010 for review after initially refusing. Therefore, regulators' stance on vaccines may be shifting. Nonetheless, risks remain.

Some Good Signs

While revenues are still under pressure, some credit should be given to Moderna for controlling cost/expenses respectably and for narrowing losses significantly. As you can see above, total operating expenses dropped by 31% YoY. This is a slightly larger decline than the revenue drop, so efficiency hasn't been a problem for them. On the bottom line, loss per share decreased from $2.91 in the year-ago quarter to $2.11 and so despite a weak top line, profitability is slowly moving in the right direction. Note that they beat on EPS by $0.49, so again things were much better than feared.

Financial Outlook Is Strong

Moderna provided some financial guidance for 2026. For the full year, they are expecting up to 10% of revenue growth. That sure beats the 40% decline we saw for full year 2025. Furthermore, as stated above, they are expecting revenues to be split 50/50 between the U.S. and international markets. Therefore, the company is expecting to gain traction in their geographic diversification. This is helping to mitigate the U.S. reliance risk I highlighted earlier.

In terms of operating expenses, they are projecting around $4.9 billion. That would imply a slight decline from 2025 levels. With revenues expected to grow but expenses expected to drop, the company is clearly making cost/expense control a priority, and investors should find that to be a highly promising sign. Profitability can be inferred to be strong from this guidance. Overall, given the multiyear growth drivers laid out earlier and the strong financial guidance for 2026, I would say that the prospects for Moderna are brightening.

Legal Risks

The other main risk to Moderna is legal disputes with other companies vis-a-vis their mRNA COVID vaccines. If the courts rule against Moderna or if settlements are significantly large, the performance of the stock may be affected. On Feb 18th, Jonathan Block of Seeking Alpha wrote that

a federal judge in Delaware has thrown out two key defenses Moderna had been claiming in its ongoing patent litigation with Arbutus Biopharma (ABUS) regarding its mRNA COVID-19 vaccine.

This ongoing legal battle is obviously something that investors don't want to see. Furthermore, just days later it was reported that BioNTech is suing Moderna for patent violation in their mNEXSPIKE vaccine that was discussed earlier. Both of these cases represent legal risks for Moderna moving forward, so despite the brightening outlook for the company, investors should keep an eye on these developments.

Valuation Analysis

With earnings being negative and revenues still declining in Q4, I believe one of the best valuation multiples to use right now is the P/B ratio. As you can see above, the multiple has started to rebound in 2026, but in my view, it still offers opportunity to investors. As of this writing, Seeking Alpha shows that their P/B ratio is at a ~16% discount to the health care sector. Furthermore, it is shown above that the multiple is still significantly below its 5-year median.

Despite the U.S. political/regulatory risks as well as the legal risks highlighted above, there are many signs that the worst is over for Moderna and that the page is finally turning for the company. Clear multiyear growth drivers and strong guidance show that the sun will shine on them again.

Even though the market has seemingly reacted to this good news, the fact that valuation is still at a relative discount suggests to me that it isn't too late to buy into the stock and that the risk/reward remains attractive now. The company is set to be a leader in mRNA, and this technology is starting to revolutionize select areas of the therapeutics industry already, so multiple expansion from here is likely in my view.

Conclusion

Moderna was a darling of the COVID era. While all-time highs are most likely out of reach in the foreseeable future, I believe the stock is worth consideration for health care sector investors. Despite the risks laid out in this article, the company is primed for a comeback now, and in my view the valuation hasn't fully captured the opportunities that they have at hand. The stock's recent rally is probably just the first sign it's waking up, and at this point, I believe it isn't too late to jump in for a recovery. As a result, I'm initiating Moderna at a buy rating.

data.title: DexCom: Gaining Favor, With Useful Tailwinds To Support It Even Further
data.description:
Background

The stock of Dexcom Inc. (DXCM), a medical equipment firm with a pioneering influence in the terrain of biosensing technology, appears to have garnered some positive interest from the market since I last explored it in mid-December 2025. If I may remind investors, when I’d reviewed the stock back then, it wasn’t having its finest hour, down by double digits on a YTD basis, and underperforming other medical equipment stocks and the US equity benchmark by quite a large margin. Since publication, it is in a healthier state, up by double digits, when the S&P500 has barely moved, and other medical device stocks have witnessed a contraction (on average).

Now after three months of a respectable performance, I expect DXCM to kick on even further. Here are three key themes supporting our bullish stance.

Gross Margin Inflection On The Cards

One underappreciated reason why I feel DXCM could potentially gain more favorable interest is because its long-suffering gross margin profile is poised to witness an inflection point this year. As you can see from the image below, gross margins have been on a relentless slump across the past 5 years and dropped to 60% last year.

This was driven by an inefficient supply chain, higher replacement costs, and lower production yields. While there were some one-off effects that won’t be reflected this, management has taken steps to resolve some of the structural drawbacks by pivoting to new suppliers, reestablishing more efficient shipping routes (thus likely driving lower freight costs), and driving higher manufacturing efficiencies. Some of these benefits were felt in the final quarter of last year, but FY26 is the year when one will finally see DXCM’s gross margins start trending higher across the whole year, with management now budgeting for +200bps worth of gross margin improvements for the year as a whole.

I don’t expect gross margin progression to halt after just one year, as customers who use their legacy G6 CGM (Continuous Glucose Monitoring) systems will have to pivot towards the G7 or G7 15 (this G7 15 product which was launched only in December 2025, offers a longer wear time of 15.5 days from the 10.5 days associated with the G7) as production of the former stops by July 1st 2026 (in their latest 10K, management has suggested that by the end of FY26 they expect all their customers to have transitioned away from G6).

Investors should consider that the G7 products (smaller form factors and all-in-one sensor/transmitter) have a less cumbersome design than G6 (larger disposable sensors and transmitters) and also have less components, which results in lower manufacturing and packaging costs all round. In addition to that, as the G7 15 penetrates even further (it has higher MARD, or Mean Absolute Relative Difference readings), at the expense of the G7 (both the G7 and the G7 17 are priced identically), it just becomes more lucrative for DXCM, as the newer 15-day product incorporates only two sensors involved for monthly use (since we have a gap of 15 days each), whereas the G7 which is 10 day sensor requires 3 sensors in a month (more sensors to manufacture, means more costs for DXCM).

Note that this tilt to the 15-day sensor set-up doesn’t just end with the G7 products; even DXCM’s newer products such as Stelo (which we discussed in our previous article), as well as the Smart Basal(the only CGM-integrated basal insulin dosing optimizer in the market, for those with type 2 diabetes is currently being rolled out in the US).

Forward Earnings Valuations Look Reasonable, While Another Year of +$1B FCF in FY26 Should Support Above-Average FCF Yields

One of the main reasons why I had supported a buy in DXCM back in December was because the stock’s valuations looked quite compelling, relative to historical standards. While the relative valuation discount isn’t as wide as what it was back then (50%) at a forward multiple of over 29x, it is still quite meaningful at 38%.

Now, some of you may argue that the medical devices stocks in general are priced at cheaper multiples; for instance, the largest medical device ETF- the iShares US Medical Devices ETF (IHI), is priced at less than 23x earnings. However, it’s also important to contextualize what sort of sustainable earnings profiles you’re getting at those lower multiples. IHI’s portfolio (which includes DXCM) is poised to deliver long-term earnings growth of less than 10%, which translates to a PEG [Price-to-earnings growth] ratio of 2.3x.

In contrast, look at DXCM’s long-term earnings profile; this is a business that is going to give you average earnings growth of roughly 20% p.a. through the next 3 years, which is double that of its peers. Put another way, DXCM’s implied PEG ratio of 1.46x, represents almost a 40% discount over the corresponding PEG ratio of its medical equipment peers.

I recognize that some investors may not be comfortable looking at valuations through the lens of earnings, given how accounting effects could potentially skew things, so how about valuations from an FCF angle?

Well, if you buy DXCM at the current market-cap, you could lock in a very useful FCF yield (trailing FCF as a function of the market-cap) of 3.63%, which is 3x better than what the stock has averaged across the past 5 years (1.22%). What’s also comforting to note is that there’s no risk of this above-average yield dropping to its long-term average due to lower free cash flow generation, as DXCM has already suggested that they will deliver another record year of +$1B worth of FCF in FY26 following what was seen in FY25 (which would translate to a yield of over 3.5% at the current market-cap).

Technicals- Risk-Reward Still Looks Favorable

Over the past month, there have been some pretty encouraging developments on DXCM’s short and long-term charts. For instance, on DXCM’s short-term daily chart, the stock has been building a base just below its 200DMA. If DXCM can manage to break beyond this and build a higher base, it could likely attract further bullish interest as the 200DMA is seen as a widely followed barometer of the long-term trend.

At the same time, recently we’ve seen bearish interest take a hit, with the total shares sold short coming off by 5% over the past month.

Meanwhile, take a look at DXCM’s long-term charts, which show the monthly movements over the last five-and-a-half years. If you join the peaks and troughs of the price movements, you have something akin to a descending channel, but within that channel, a prospective long position at the current market price doesn’t look too shabby given the risk reward on offer (a downside gap of 30%, as opposed to an upside gap of 70%).

Another reason why I think a long position in DXCM could be fruitful is because the stock’s relative strength ratio versus an equally weighted basket of healthcare stocks from the S&P 500 is trading below its long-term mean (a 35% gap) and could be due some upward mean reversion.

Closing Thoughts

I reiterate my buy rating on the DXCM stock as its long-suffering gross margin profile is set to embark on a more fruitful period, its valuations - both from an earnings and a free cash flow basis - looks reasonable, and the risk-reward on its charts looks favorable.

data.title: Expect Less In 2026 - But Don't Bet Against CGDV
data.description:
The Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (CGDV) is a core portfolio candidate, and neither a true dividend nor a true value proposition. This is a filtered version of SPY with an emphasis on quality. This blended (more tilted value, but decently growth oriented within the large cap group) active strategy has been ahead of the broader markets for a few years now. So its recent outperformance against SPY does not raise eyebrows, as did the case of MGV I recently write about. CGDV's thesis is not about value versus growth ahead - it never has been. CGDV's Buy thesis rests on growth in quality large caps. That should hold up well in 2026 and relative performance against SPY is likely to stay intact. However, we may see a more subdued year overall in absolute return terms.

Methodology and Portfolio Clarifications

CGDV's name suggests dividend value. The website shows the weighted average portfolio more accurately classified as blended, but still shows a tilt toward value. The truth is that dividend-paying stocks make up the core of the portfolio and serve only as a gating variable check for entry - the quality focus likely stops there. The large cap bias is also evident and adds to the quality focus versus SPY.

Valuation metrics do not show expected value characteristics. The P/E discount versus SPY is modest (~8%). The P/CF ratio is only slightly higher than the S&P 500. The P/B ratio reflects a discount but that is likely the effect of profitability focus rather than valuations. CGDV is not definitely low multiple investing - it is slightly cheaper than SPY (potentially classifiable as GARP) but still very much a growth strategy at heart.

The portfolio holds most of the tech mega-caps that are in the top 10 SPY Holdings, but the allocations hold less weight. CGDV underweights Amazon, Apple, Alphabet and Nvidia significantly and reallocates weights to select growth infrastructure names like Broadcom, Applied Materials and GE. It also weeds out speculative growth names (like Tesla) to a large extent. The overall portfolio is heavily concentrated. The top 10 have a similar ~36% combined weight. However, the portfolio size (number of holdings) is almost a tenth of SPY, implying narrower exposure and a missing tail in lower market cap names.

CGDV achieves its growth tilt without similar emphasis in terms of weight on tech. The tech quality has been conducive though with greater emphasis on AI themes and semis and less exposure to software. Unlike traditional value-focused strategies, CGDV is dramatically underweight financials. The real tilt versus SPY is in industrials, energy, healthcare and material - sectors that have also seen secular growth in the current cycle, especially if right selection calls were made.

Overall, CGDV, unlike its name suggests, is a SPY peer with a quality tilt. Income is part of the story, and more likely a selection criterion proxying for quality. Growth in CGDV thrives when quality mega caps win over speculative growth. This is a core portfolio allocation.

Performance and Reasons

I look at CGDV's historical performance to understand the core drivers and trends that can be extrapolated ahead. First of all, CGDV's outperformance versus SPY is not a recent development. There has been a sustained performance gap since 2023, only enhanced in the bull run post April 2025 and still continuing as broader markets have consolidated in the past 3-4 months.

This structural and persistent gap comes from the portfolio. We have discussed some aspect of that in the previous section. Such as the bet on quality tech and a lack of software and speculative tech growth names that have worked. CGDV has accumulated its alpha through other sector positioning too. The overweight healthcare and industrials positioning has allowed it to capture growth from GLP-1 leadership trends, infrastructure spending tailwinds and defense and aerospace strength. Energy as a theme has also seen growth in pockets due to electrification and AI demand - something SPY has not been able to capture as much. The underweight in financials means less drag that dilutes growth elsewhere as has potentially happened in the case of SPY.

The Road Ahead

So that brings us to the critical point around what to expect in 2026. Let us break that down into two sub-questions. The first question to be answered is absolute expectations and the second is around its relative performance versus SPY.

The relative outperformance scenario is still supportive for CGDV, which is why I rate it a Buy. CGDV needs AI leadership to be broad at the top - that is narrative-based smaller caps should not outperform and at the same time AI mega caps at the top don't concentrate growth in 1-2 names SPY is overweight on. The second outcome cannot be ruled out entirely, and a couple of mega caps could support SPY over CGDV, but if growth is more spread out across the Magnificent 7, CGDV should be able to capture some of that growth.

In terms of vertical segments, semis and hardware over software should remain the theme and that is likely supported because of two factors that are already playing out. The software sector has undergone severe corrections already and could mechanically rebound a bit. However, I do not see much structural growth there because the question is around how AI disrupts the space and commoditizes some of the offerings and reduces pricing power. That answer will likely need time to be answered, so software will await long term clarity over headline metrics. At the same time, AI hardware and infra, especially semis, will remain supported in the near term because of the historically elevated capex spend levels we are seeing. That actually dampens the free cash flow of those in the AI race, but means business for the infrastructure layer. CGDV is well positioned in terms of both these outlooks.

The industrials allocation should also remain strong, if not deliver further alpha in 2026. Defense spending should be strong, given the geopolitical tensions in play. The only area we need to keep a watch on is PMI improvements and steady capital goods demand - not a concern right now.

In healthcare, we should continue to see relative strength in GLP-1 momentum, even if biotech risk appetite does not rebound strongly in an environment where long duration risk appetite is visibly slowing. The energy theme also looks relatively good if oil prices remain above marginal cost of production and if there is no deep global recession. The odds of recession in the US have meaningfully compressed from ~60% odds in 2025 to ~40% odds - not a non-issue, but a lower probability outcome. Energy is also well-supported due to AI power demand tailwinds.

Other factors that could have tilted the total return outlook in favor of SPY like a broader market rally (smaller caps) is unlikely to happen unless rates fall meaningfully and liquidity rises, or economic growth rebounds strongly - not a scenario expected in 2026. Overall, the markets will likely continue to favor quality premium and CGDV should hold its lead.

However, there is the other important sub-question around absolute growth expectations. I expect CGDV to have a significantly flatter year in 2026 (mid-single digit outlook) than the rise in 2025. And this is because of the fact that growth investing is currently cooling and is likely to remain so until earnings begin to see upgrade momentum - something that is not happening today. And unlike value plays like MGV, which I recently wrote about, CGDV does not have rotation trends to rely on to make for an outsized return thesis, despite a slower growth environment.

The summary therefore reads like this. CGDV is likely to keep its lead over SPY ahead. It is a Buy both in relative and absolute terms. However, the absolute growth expectations should be subdued compared to its recent past - a Buy with tempered expectations.

data.title: AI Buildout Will Keep The Broad Bull Market Intact
data.description:
The last several months have been exceedingly frustrating ones for stock market bears, who have strained their patience waiting for a “correction” in the major indexes that has thus far eluded them. As measured by the S&P 500 index, the market has, up until now, refused to comply with the bears’ expectations—even with the latest flare-up in the Middle East.

Granted that a market decline could occur at any time in the present environment, my contention is that any setback in the broad U.S. equity in the coming days and weeks is likely to be very short-lived. That’s the case I’ll attempt to prove out here, and I believe the weight of evidence supports it. And while the evidence I’ll cite is partly technical in nature, it ultimately revolves around the ongoing artificial intelligence buildout being the main support for keeping stock prices elevated across several major industries.

To begin with, let’s take a look at what I consider the most salient aspects of the current bull market. First, the key components include the financial and tech sectors; specifically, banks, broker/dealers, and semiconductors.

The importance of these sectors is clear enough, with the banks and broker/dealers serving as barometers of financial sector strength, as well as the magnitude of liquidity in the market (which is one of the most important considerations for the broad market’s overall health). As for the semiconductor stocks, this group is not only an excellent reflection of the health of the overall tech sector, but I think it’s also safe to say that chip stock strength reflects the progression of the AI buildout.

Despite interest rate volatility, tariff-related angst, and geopolitical worries, U.S. bank and broker/dealer stocks have held up very admirably so far this year. Consider the KBW Bank Index (BKX), which many analysts view as the prime benchmark for the bank stocks in the aggregate.

It’s true that BKX has shown some weakness over the last few days, as reflected in the chart below, but its main trend has been strong since the last major correction almost a year ago. And while a test of the widely watched 200-day moving average (the red line in the chart shown here) is indeed a strong possibility in the coming days, even if this trend line is broken, I don’t envision the bank stocks remaining under prolonged selling pressure.

Underpinning this view is the mostly sanguine view for 2026 expressed among banking executives at a recent Piper Sandler-sponsored financial services conference. According to the presenters at this conference (held in November), the banking sector is expected to outperform this year compared to 2025 due to a variety of trends, including improved credit. Quoting from a recently published overview of the conference:

Bank management teams are positioning constructively for 2026, citing disruption-driven hiring, diminishing commercial real estate lending drag, stable to upward trending margins, a favorable regulatory environment, and robust capital return dynamics as key factors supporting their outlook.”

The article further stated that Piper Sandler viewed core credit trends as remaining “extremely strong…with banks well-reserved and well-capitalized, with actual loss content not matching recent negative speculation.”

On the subject of the improving credit outlook, it’s also worth noting that liquidity has been very strong so far this year, thanks in large part to the Federal Reserve’s recent bond-buying operations. Specifically, the Fed has been steadily buying U.S. Treasuries—about $40 billion per month—prompting some Fed watchers to call it “QE5” (and to be fair, the Fed isn’t calling it quantitative easing).

But as the Fed’s bond-buying operations are expected to continue until at least mid-April, I believe this will continue to keep the major stock market indexes buoyant through the spring, since it provides an extra injection of liquidity (the lifeblood of any bull market).

As far as the impact the Iran/Israel war is having on the equity market, the venerable Dr. Ed Yardeni was interviewed this week on Bloomberg and stated his opinion that “for all practical purposes, this war is over for Iran.” He also expressed his view that the market is already looking beyond this geopolitical event and may be viewing it as potentially increasing stability in the Middle East, with stocks likely having already discounted this outcome.

Turning to the semiconductors, the PHLX Semiconductor Index (SOX) presents an even better picture of where the broad market currently stands. My observation has always been that “as go the semis, so goes the Nasdaq, so goes the broad market.” In other words, SOX strength typically translates into categorical strength due to the outsized importance of this particular industry.

Unlike the bank stocks, chip stocks, as reflected by the SOX chart (below), are very close to their yearly highs and well above the 200-day moving average—and in spite of increased investor anxiety over the semiconductor stocks at large.

With semiconductors more important today than ever as it pertains to the AI and data center buildouts, the momentum behind the semis (at least in my estimation) is the fact that chip demand remains robust as the buildout trend continues. On that score, Nvidia’s (NVDA) recent blowout earnings report and massive fiscal Q1 revenue guidance have been interpreted by industry analysts as “the AI chip boom is still accelerating.”

In terms of potential risk to my thesis, if the current Iran/Israel/U.S. conflict isn’t resolved in a timely fashion and extends beyond the four-to-five-week timeframe to victory the White House and other military experts are projecting right now, there could be extended volatility in the energy markets. This in turn could exert a headwind on stocks and keep the major indexes from making progress in the coming months; it could even contribute to sustained selling pressure in equities.

Also, a failure of the Fed to continue its present measures of providing additional liquidity to the market beyond the projected mid-April period (in terms of its bond-buying operations) might serve to deter robust participation from institutional investors, with the possible result that the bears would find it easier to successfully “raid” the more vulnerable sectors of the market.

That said, I believe the combination of 1.) a worst-case scenario of the latest Middle East flare-up likely already discounted by the market, 2.) abundant liquidity thanks to the Fed’s bond-buying operations, 3.) an improved banking and credit outlook for 2026, and 4.) ongoing data center and AI buildout strength as reflected in the semiconductor stock will contribute to ongoing strength in the broad market.

As such, I’m planning to stay fully invested in equities regardless of any near-term volatility that might rear its head in the coming days. I see the broad market’s uptrend remaining intact heading into the spring months, with the bears once again having to exercise long patience for their day to arrive.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Kevin Warsh's Job Just Got Harder
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Bond market signals rising inflation worries ahead of Kevin Warsh’s Fed chairmanship. (0:15) ISM services index jumps to three-year high. (1:09) Crypto-linked stocks rally after Trump urges banks to work with industry. (1:55)

The following is an abridged transcript:

Fed-chief-to-be Kevin Warsh’s job is getting tougher by the day.

The White House may want lower fed funds rates, but the bond market is signaling rising inflation worries after the attacks on Iran and the surge in oil prices.

Trump has said the conflict could last four to five weeks.

That leaves a couple of months for things to normalize before Warsh’s first meeting — though Polymarket odds currently predict a cessation of hostilities by the end of June.

Fed funds futures now show a 60% chance rates stay unchanged after Warsh’s inaugural meeting — up from 54% a week ago and 42% a month ago. Two quarter-point cuts are still priced in by year-end, though the odds have dipped to around 60%.

Minneapolis Fed President Neel Kashkari said it’s “too soon to know” what impact a war with Iran might have on inflation or how long any effects could last.

“Coming into this moment, I was feeling pretty good,”he said. “I felt like policy was in a pretty good place.”

On the economic front, the ISM Services Index jumped to 56.1 in February from 53.8 in January, beating the 53.5 consensus.

It’s the highest level in more than three years and marks the 20th-straight month of expansion.

Economists at Wells Fargo say some service-sector firms are adding headcount in anticipation of stronger activity.

And while higher prices remain a burden, the ISM data suggest cost pressures aren’t getting materially worse. Their bottom line: service-sector conditions are improving as businesses navigate steep uncertainty.

And ahead of Friday’s jobs report, ADP says private-sector employment grew by 63K in February — topping the 43K consensus.

That’s roughly in line with what economists expect from the official numbers, though ADP’s track record is spotty.

Among active stocks, crypto exchange names — including Coinbase Global (COIN), Galaxy Digital (GLXY), bitcoin miners, and other crypto-linked stocks — rallied after President Donald Trump urged banks to “make a good deal” with the crypto industry to advance stalled legislation.

A major sticking point for progress on the crypto bill remains Wall Street’s objection to stablecoins that feature yield-like payments.

CrowdStrike (CRWD) is choppy post-earnings, but it got plenty of love from Wall Street.

RBC’s Matthew Hedberg said the company “delivered a very strong quarter by accelerating at scale and raising FY27 ARR expectations.”

He added that even with the raised outlook, “upside is likely throughout the year.”

And Moderna (MRNA) continued to rise as analysts said its agreement to resolve a patent dispute with Arbutus Biopharma (ABUS) and Roivant’s Genevant unit (ROIV) removed a key legal overhang for the COVID vaccine maker.

Moderna agreed to pay $950M to settle the dispute related to delivery technology behind its vaccine.

In other news of note, whale to the rescue.

Billionaire Leo KoGuan, one of the largest individual shareholders of Tesla (TSLA), said he bought 1M shares of Nvidia (NVDA).

“I am convinced AI is NOT a bubble — it is only the beginning,” he posted.

He told Bloomberg he plans to buy another 1M Nvidia shares soon “to show support to a nervous market.”

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, Brown Brothers Harriman says institutional investors are preparing to expand their exposure to exchange traded funds — even as market volatility pushes portfolio managers to refocus on strategies aimed at generating alpha.

In the firm’s Global ETF Investor Survey, 96% of respondents said they plan to increase ETF allocations over the next 12 months — matching last year’s record level.

“ETF issuers are innovating to meet the evolving needs of today’s investors,” said Tim Huver, managing director on ETF Servicing.

He added that the next phase of growth will likely emphasize active management, broader asset-class access, and more sophisticated operational capabilities.
